input_text:
Voyager 1 began its voyage from Cape Canaveral on September 5, 1977. This deployment occurred sixteen days after its companion, Voyager 2, a difference that might have surprised observers since NASA had planned unique paths for the two probes. The primary mission, which spanned five years, launched aboard a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket. The venture's objective was to conduct close flybys of Jupiter and Saturn, capitalizing on a rare planetary alignment that recurs approximately every 176 years.

The probe encountered Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this visit, it successfully relayed the first high-resolution images revealing the planet’s faint ring structure and documented active volcanoes on its moon, Io, which marked the initial observation of volcanism outside of Earth. Two years later, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 reached Saturn, transmitting magnificent photographs of its rings and detailed views of Titan. The subsequent trajectory toward Titan guided Voyager 1 beyond the plane of the solar system, concluding its phase of planetary science while simultaneously setting a course toward interstellar space.

Following a suggestion from astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 directed its cameras back toward Earth on February 14, 1990, capturing an image of our planet when it was situated about 6 billion kilometers away. This solitary picture, which appeared as a single pixel against the Sun's diminished radiation, gained enduring fame as the Pale Blue Dot. This photograph represented the final capture by the spacecraft before the cameras were shut down to conserve its waning power supply.

On August 25, 2012, Voyager 1 entered the heliopause, thus becoming the first human-made object to enter interstellar space. The mission includes the Golden Record—a 12-inch, gold-plated copper phonograph disc assembled by Sagan—which contains greetings in 55 languages, chosen music, and visuals intended for any civilization it might encounter. Its ongoing operation is sustained by three radioisotope thermoelectric generators powered by plutonium-238, whose half-life of 87.7 years has gradually reduced the spacecraft's energy.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is approximately 25 billion kilometers distant from Earth, maintaining a velocity nearing 61,000 kilometers per hour. Furthermore, projections suggest that by November 2026, it will become the first probe to cover a distance equivalent to one light-day.
output_text:
Voyager 1 embarked on its journey from Cape Canaveral on September 5, 1977. This launch took place sixteen days after its partner, Voyager 2, a timing discrepancy that might have been unexpected because NASA had designed distinct paths for the two probes. The mission, which spanned five years, was launched using a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket. The core purpose of this endeavor was to execute close flybys of both Jupiter and Saturn, taking advantage of a rare planetary alignment that happens roughly every 176 years.

The probe arrived at Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this transit, it successfully relayed the first high-resolution images showing the planet's subtle ring structure and documented active volcanoes on its moon, Io, marking the initial observation of volcanism outside of Earth. Two years later, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 reached Saturn, sending back stunning photographs of its rings and detailed views of Titan. The following course toward Titan propelled Voyager 1 past the plane of the solar system, concluding its planetary science phase while simultaneously charting a course toward interstellar space.

At the suggestion of astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 turned its instruments back toward Earth on February 14, 1990, capturing an image of our planet when it was approximately 6 billion kilometers away. This single picture, which appeared as a solitary pixel against the Sun's diminishing radiation, became famously known as the Pale Blue Dot. This photograph constituted the last capture by the spacecraft before the cameras were powered down to conserve its dwindling energy supply.

On August 25, 2012, Voyager 1 entered the heliopause, thereby achieving the distinction of being the first human artifact to reach interstellar space. The mission also includes the Golden Record—a 12-inch, gold-plated copper phonograph disc assembled by Sagan—containing greetings in 55 languages, selected music, and visuals intended for any civilization it might meet. Its continued operation is maintained by three radioisotope thermoelectric generators fueled by plutonium-238, whose half-life of 87.7 years has progressively diminished the spacecraft's power.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is situated about 25 billion kilometers from Earth, traveling at a speed approaching 61,000 kilometers per hour. Moreover, projections indicate that by November 2026, it will be the first probe to cover a distance equal to one light-day.
